critic_raw: The CNN for early-stage lung cancer detection experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the CNN's decision threshold is tuned to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity, making sensitivity-equal results uninformative — the CNN achieves the same sensitivity by construction. Second, multi-architecture search on the validation set (3 architectures) inflates expected validation performance of the selected architecture; no correction for selection bias is applied.
all_issues_raised: CNN threshold is tuned to match radiologist sensitivity, making sensitivity-equal results uninformative about detection superiority; comparison conflates threshold calibration with model quality.
Multi-architecture search (3 candidates) on validation set inflates expected validation AUROC of the selected architecture relative to test; no correction for model selection bias is applied.

round_transcripts.critic: The CNN for early-stage lung cancer detection experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the CNN's decision threshold is tuned to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity, making sensitivity-equal results uninformative — the CNN achieves the same sensitivity by construction. Second, multi-architecture search on the validation set (3 architectures) inflates expected validation performance of the selected architecture; no correction for selection bias is applied.
round_transcripts.defender: On threshold selection: the design uses the validation set to select the threshold, which is standard. The comparison is operationally motivated: the clinical standard is radiologist sensitivity, and the question is whether the CNN can match or exceed that at an operationally relevant specificity. The AUROC metric (secondary) provides a threshold-free comparison. If AUROC is higher for the CNN, it is a better discriminator regardless of threshold. On multi-architecture selection: testing three architectures is common in applied deep learning research. The selection inflation concern is real but modest with three candidates; the difference between the best and median validation AUROC is unlikely to cause the selected model to fail dramatically on test. The design includes secondary metrics (AUROC, calibration) that would reveal any selection-inflated test performance.

round_transcripts.critic: On threshold selection: the defense notes AUROC is a secondary metric providing threshold-free comparison. This partially addresses the concern — if CNN AUROC is higher than radiologist AUROC, the CNN is better. However, radiologist AUROC is not a well-defined metric (radiologists do not produce continuous scores in the same way). The comparison is therefore asymmetric: CNN has a continuous score and computed AUROC, while radiologist performance is measured as a point (sensitivity at implicit specificity). The test design cannot compute radiologist AUROC. The threshold-free comparison the defense invokes is not actually available for the human baseline. On multi-architecture selection: three candidates produce modest inflation, but the design should at minimum report which architectures were tested and their individual validation AUROCs to allow readers to assess selection pressure.
round_transcripts.defender: On radiologist AUROC: the defense concedes that radiologist AUROC cannot be directly computed since radiologists report binary decisions, not continuous scores. The comparison is therefore necessarily at a fixed operating point. The threshold-matching approach makes the sensitivity comparison fair at one operating point; AUROC comparison is available only for the CNN. The design should acknowledge this asymmetry explicitly. On multi-architecture selection: reporting all candidate architectures' validation AUROCs alongside the selected architecture is good practice and should be added to the design. The inflation risk is modest but transparency requires it.

all_issues_adjudicated: CNN-vs-radiologist comparison is fundamentally asymmetric: CNN has continuous scores and AUROC, radiologist performance is a binary decision point; threshold-matching does not resolve this asymmetry and it should be acknowledged in the design's scope.
